The Problem

Most social programs don't fail from lack of intention.

They fail because no one owned the execution — the systems, the tracking, the stakeholder coordination, the documentation that keeps funders engaged and programs running.

If any of the following sound familiar, you're not alone. And you're exactly who I work with.

"That gap between strategy and execution? That's exactly where I work."

01
Your program stalls between planning and delivery.

The idea is approved, the funding is secured — but six months later you're still untangling roles, timelines, and who owns what. Nobody is driving execution end-to-end.

02
Your donor reports describe activities, not outcomes.

You can account for money spent but struggle to demonstrate measurable change. You know it's costing you renewal conversations, but the M&E infrastructure isn't there to fix it.

03
Your team is stretched across programs that aren't coordinated.

Deadlines slip. Partners go quiet. Staff are doing the work of three people and producing the output of half. The problem isn't effort — it's structure.

04
You've hired consultants who delivered decks and disappeared.

You got a beautiful framework, a handover call, and were left to figure out implementation alone. What you needed was someone who would stay, build the systems, and see it through.

05
Your KPIs are vague enough that success and failure look the same.

On paper, the program ran. In reality, nobody can prove it moved the needle — and your next funding round depends on evidence you don't have.

Services

Three Ways I Close the Gap Between Strategy and Results

01

From Idea to Fundable Program

I take your concept — or your problem statement — and build it into a structured, executable program with a Theory of Change, LogFrame, workplan, and budget. Everything a funder or your own board needs to say yes and move forward with confidence.

  • Approved Theory of Change and LogFrame, ready for submission
  • Detailed workplan with milestones, owners, and dependencies
  • Donor-ready program proposal or full program document
02

Proof Your Program Is Working

I design the MEL systems that track what matters — not just activities, but outcomes. You get KPI dashboards, data collection tools, and impact reports that hold up under donor scrutiny and support your next renewal conversation.

  • M&E framework with indicators, baselines, and targets
  • Data collection tools calibrated to your beneficiary group
  • Mid-term and end-of-project reports that demonstrate measurable change
03

Execution That Doesn't Break Down

I manage the operational infrastructure that keeps programs on track — vendor procurement, partner coordination, workflow systems, and stakeholder communication. Your team stays aligned, timelines hold, and nothing falls through the cracks.

  • Procurement plans and end-to-end vendor management
  • Stakeholder engagement strategy and communication protocols
  • Operational SOPs and internal management systems your team can own
Proof & Results

Numbers That Come From the Field, Not a Slide Deck

25K+
Sanitary Pads Distributed · Pick A Pad

Menstrual Health at Scale

With no pre-existing organization and starting from a blank page, I co-founded Pick A Pad and designed its full operations — distribution systems, volunteer coordination, M&E frameworks, and partner agreements. Over 3 years, we reached 8+ schools and community sites across Lagos with a 60% measured improvement in beneficiary health awareness and 100% on-time program delivery across every cycle.

750
Adolescent Girls Supported · Project RISE

A 21-Day Multi-Intervention Program

Bloom Foundation needed a funded program run end-to-end — Theory of Change, LogFrame, budget, field coordination, mentorship, mental health access, school fee support, and educational materials, all delivered across 2 schools in 21 days. I owned the full project lifecycle, from design to live documentation to donor reporting, reaching 750 adolescent girls with a structured, evidence-based intervention.

20%
Faster Execution · ReadWrite Data Solutions

Operational Systems That Actually Stick

Managing 5+ concurrent projects with cross-functional teams of 8–12, I implemented Agile systems (Scrum/Kanban) using Jira, Notion, and Asana that cut average project execution time by 20% and drove a 95%+ on-time delivery rate. By restructuring stakeholder reporting and building visibility dashboards, blockers were caught early and client communication improved across the board.

These aren't hypothetical case studies — they're the scale and rigor you're hiring into your next program.

About

Strategic Partner. Not Another Consultant.

I'm Chisom Melvina Ugochukwu — a Social Impact Project Manager and NGO Co-Founder with 3+ years of experience delivering community development, menstrual health, and youth empowerment programs across Nigeria.

I co-founded Pick A Pad, built its strategy, operations, and volunteer systems from scratch, and have since worked with organizations including Bloom Foundation, Docktor Connect, and ReadWrite Data Solutions to manage programs, coordinate stakeholders, and deliver measurable results.

What I bring to your program isn't a template and a handover call. It's operational ownership — the systems, documentation, coordination, and reporting your program needs to succeed and be renewed. I work remotely and am open to project contracts, retainers, and international engagements.

FAQ

Questions Worth Answering Before You Reach Out

If you've been burned by a consultant before, these are the questions that matter.

Send a Direct Brief
What engagement models do you offer? +
I work in two main structures. Project-based engagements are scoped to a specific deliverable — a LogFrame, an M&E framework, a program proposal, or an end-of-program evaluation report. Retainer engagements work better for organizations that need embedded, ongoing project management support across one or multiple programs. Both structures are available for international clients and can be structured with milestone-based payments.
How does a typical engagement start? +
You send me a brief — a few sentences about your organization, your program, and what you need. Within 48 hours I'll come back with a direct response: whether I think I'm the right fit, what an engagement would look like structurally, and what the next step is. No discovery calls that go nowhere. No decks you didn't ask for.
What makes you different from other project management consultants? +
Most project management consultants bring frameworks. I bring field experience — I co-founded an NGO, ran a funded program supporting 750 adolescent girls, and have managed multi-program portfolios with real accountability. I don't hand over a document and leave. I understand what it takes to move work from paper to community, and I build systems your team can use after I'm gone.
Which sectors and program areas do you work in? +
My deepest experience is in girls' education, menstrual health, youth empowerment, and community development. That said, the project management and MEL skills I bring are transferable — I've also worked in digital health and data services. If you're running a structured program with clear beneficiary groups, donor accountability, and a need for measurable outcomes, we're likely a strong fit.
What results can I realistically expect? +
That depends on what you bring me and what stage you're at. At minimum: a structurally sound program with clear indicators, documented processes, and reporting you can submit to funders without embarrassment. In longer engagements: an organization that runs more efficiently, with accountability systems that reduce delays, and outcome data that supports your next funding conversation. I don't promise outcomes I can't control. I do promise rigorous execution on my end.
Do you work remotely with international clients? +
Yes — fully. I'm based in Lagos, Nigeria, and available for remote engagements with organizations globally. I work across time zones and am experienced in managing stakeholder communication, documentation, and reporting remotely. Where field presence is required, we can discuss what that means for the engagement structure.
Is this just advice, or do you produce actual deliverables? +
Deliverables. Every engagement produces something tangible — a workplan, an M&E framework, a budget, a donor report, a set of SOPs. These are documents your team can use, your donors can review, and your board can approve. You're not paying for opinions. You're paying for structured, submission-ready outputs.
How quickly can you start? +
That depends on my current availability and your timeline. I'm currently open to new engagements — reach out with your brief and I'll give you an honest answer about start dates and fit within 48 hours.
